questions.question: When using expo-location's watchPositionAsync for a ride-sharing driver tracking feature, why must you always call subscription.remove() on component unmount?
options: Forgetting to call remove() causes the GPS coordinates to be stored in AsyncStorage indefinitely.

watchPositionAsync registers a native GPS listener that continues running independently of React's component lifecycle. Without calling remove() on unmount: (1) the listener continues consuming battery tracking location even when the driver goes to a different screen, (2) the JS callback fires on an unmounted component warning, and (3) multiple subscriptions stack if the component remounts, causing duplicate handlers and excessive battery drain.
The subscription automatically removes itself when the user backgrounds the app.
remove() is optional — it only matters for accelerometer subscriptions, not location.
questions.answer: watchPositionAsync registers a native GPS listener that continues running independently of React's component lifecycle. Without calling remove() on unmount: (1) the listener continues consuming battery tracking location even when the driver goes to a different screen, (2) the JS callback fires on an unmounted component warning, and (3) multiple subscriptions stack if the component remounts, causing duplicate handlers and excessive battery drain.
questions.explanation: Battery impact: GPS is one of the highest battery consumers on mobile. A ride-sharing app that forgets subscription.remove() on the route screen will drain the driver's battery even when they're viewing their earnings screen. Always pair watchPositionAsync with useEffect cleanup: return () => subscription?.remove().

questions.question: Why must you handle the case where requestMediaLibraryPermissionsAsync returns 'denied' even if the user previously granted permission?
options: Permissions expire after 30 days and must be re-requested.

Users can revoke permissions at any time in iOS/Android Settings without the app knowing. Between permission checks, a user may have visited Settings and turned off Photos access. Your app must check permission status each time before attempting to use a protected resource, gracefully handle the denied case (show an explanation with a Settings deeplink rather than silently failing), and never assume previously granted permissions remain granted.
requestMediaLibraryPermissionsAsync always returns 'denied' on the first call.
Android requires re-requesting all permissions after an app update.
questions.answer: Users can revoke permissions at any time in iOS/Android Settings without the app knowing. Between permission checks, a user may have visited Settings and turned off Photos access. Your app must check permission status each time before attempting to use a protected resource, gracefully handle the denied case (show an explanation with a Settings deeplink rather than silently failing), and never assume previously granted permissions remain granted.
questions.explanation: Best practice pattern: check permission → if denied AND canAskAgain → prompt for permission. If denied AND !canAskAgain (user selected 'Don't ask again') → Alert.alert with a 'Open Settings' button that calls Linking.openSettings(). Never silently fail — users are confused when a button does nothing without explanation.

questions.question: Apple Sign-In only provides the user's name and email on the FIRST sign-in. Why is this critical to handle correctly in your backend?
options: Apple Sign-In is optional for non-social apps — this restriction only applies to social features.

Apple's privacy design intentionally limits data sharing: full name and email are sent once on first authentication. If your backend doesn't save them immediately (or your save fails and you retry the Apple auth), subsequent sign-ins only provide an opaque user ID. You must implement: (1) save name/email on the backend on first receipt, (2) don't rely on subsequent Apple auth responses to have this data, (3) possibly offer users a way to add their info manually if the initial save fails.
Apple provides name and email on every sign-in — the 'first time only' applies only to the email.
The email is always available; only the name is restricted to first sign-in.
questions.answer: Apple's privacy design intentionally limits data sharing: full name and email are sent once on first authentication. If your backend doesn't save them immediately (or your save fails and you retry the Apple auth), subsequent sign-ins only provide an opaque user ID. You must implement: (1) save name/email on the backend on first receipt, (2) don't rely on subsequent Apple auth responses to have this data, (3) possibly offer users a way to add their info manually if the initial save fails.
questions.explanation: Real production bug: auth succeeds, backend crashes during user creation, name/email lost forever. The user re-authenticates with Apple but your backend only receives their Apple user ID — you create a nameless account. Prevention: wrap the user creation in a transaction, or show a name entry form as a fallback onboarding step.
